title: Lentil and feta salad
subtitle: Puy lentils dressed warm with red wine vinegar and topped with feta.
excerpt: Puy lentils cooked until just tender, then dressed warm with red wine vinegar, olive oil, and crushed garlic. Topped with crumbled feta, cherry tomatoes, and flat-leaf parsley. Serves as a main or a substantial side.
sourceNotes: Warm lentil salads dressed with vinegar and oil are a Lyon and Auvergne bistro standard, where lentilles du Puy carry an AOC. The combination of warm lentils with vinegar dressing crossed into British home cooking in the 1990s; the addition of feta reflects the broader Mediterranean influence on British domestic cookery from that period onwards.

content.text: Warm lentils absorb vinegar and oil the way warm potatoes absorb mayonnaise: more deeply than cold ones do. The technique is the same as a French bistro salade de lentilles. Dress the lentils as soon as they are drained, while still hot, and leave them to come to room temperature in the dressing before adding anything else.

Puy lentils hold their shape after cooking where red or yellow lentils collapse. That texture, firm individual grains staying separate, is what makes this a salad rather than something closer to a soup.
A medium saucepan, colander, and large mixing bowl.

Put {{puy-lentils}} of rinsed Puy lentils in a saucepan with the bay leaves. Cover with cold water by about 5 cm. Bring to a boil, then reduce to a steady simmer and cook for 20 to 25 minutes until the lentils are tender but still hold their shape. Drain and discard the bay leaves.

Whisk {{olive-oil}} of olive oil, {{red-wine-vinegar}} of red wine vinegar, {{dijon-mustard}} of Dijon mustard, and the crushed garlic together in a large bowl. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt and {{black-pepper}} of black pepper. While the lentils are still hot, tip them into the dressing and toss well. Leave to cool to room temperature, about 20 minutes.

Add the cherry tomatoes, red onion, and parsley to the cooled lentils. Toss gently. Scatter the crumbled feta over the top just before serving. Do not mix the feta in. It will dissolve into the dressing if stirred.

items.fix: Use Puy lentils specifically. They hold their shape better than green or brown lentils. Check for tenderness at 18 minutes.
items.cause: Overcooked, or a variety other than Puy was used.

items.fix: Add a drizzle more olive oil and leave for five minutes. The balance will shift as the lentils continue to absorb.
items.cause: Too much vinegar relative to the oil, or the lentils absorbed the dressing unevenly.

items.fix: Add feta only when the lentils are at room temperature, and place it on top rather than stirring it through.
items.cause: Feta was added while the lentils were still warm.
items.symptom: Feta has dissolved into the dressing

items.fix: Refresh with a splash of red wine vinegar and a drizzle of olive oil before serving. Taste and adjust.
items.cause: Lentils have absorbed all the dressing overnight.

content.text: The warm lentil salad is a French bistro staple that crossed into British cooking with ease. It needs no skill beyond boiling and dressing. Puy lentils, grown in the volcanic soils of the Auvergne, became common in British shops and are now standard. The feta is not French. It is the British home version of the dish: a practical add-on that makes the salad filling enough for lunch rather than a side.
